book_summary: Exodus has chapters 1-24 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The completed material runs from names and oppression through liberation, wilderness formation, Sinai covenant preparation, Ten Words, covenant ordinances, and covenant ratification. Key themes include YHWH's seeing/hearing/knowing, liberation from forced service, Pesach/Passover, manna/Sabbath enoughness, water from rock, Jethro's distributed leadership, covenant identity as priestly kingdom/holy nation, liberation-shaped Torah/instruction, justice for vulnerable people, and covenant fellowship before Divine.

translation_notes: Hebrew-first approach required: Shemot/Names, YHWH, Elohim, avad/avodah, berit, ruach, pesach, mishkan, kavod, chesed where relevant.
Avad/avodah is a central thread: Pharaoh turns service into crushing bondage; YHWH liberates for covenant service/worship.
YHWH name disclosure in Exodus 3:14 preserves Hebrew verbal ambiguity: I AM / I WILL BE.
Pharaoh hardening language must track Hebrew verbs carefully: hazaq, kaved, qashah require later lexical audit.
Do not demonize Egyptians ethnically; critique Pharaoh, empire, forced labor, and fear-based domination.
Women's agency is central in Exodus 1-2 and should remain visible.
Speech/disability material must not shame Moses; the narrative gives presence, teaching, and Aaron as accommodation/partnership.
Pesach/Passover should be preserved as Hebrew liberation memorial, not flattened into later theology before Exodus is heard on its own terms.
Mixed multitude in Exodus 12:38 is crucial for liberation's multi-people dimension.
Sea of Reeds crossing is liberation from chariot power, not ethnic hatred.
Miriam is explicitly prophetess and leads women with timbrels and dances; preserve her leadership.
Manna economy emphasizes enoughness: much had no excess and little had no lack.
Sabbath appears as gift before Sinai law code; rest is liberation practice.
Jethro is a non-Israelite priest whose wisdom protects Moses and Israel from burnout; preserve outside wisdom and distributed leadership.
Sinai covenant begins with liberation and eagle-wing rescue before command.
Ten Words should be framed as liberation-shaped covenant instruction, not abstract legalism.
Ancient slavery, patriarchy, violence, and property laws in Exodus 21-23 require source preservation plus anti-domination safeguards. They must not be treated as timeless moral ideals.
Sojourner ethics are grounded in knowing the soul/life of the sojourner from Egypt memory.
Land promise and displacement language require later ethical audit and must not be used to justify modern dispossession or violence.
Covenant blood/life in Exodus 24 is ritual life-symbolism around covenant words, response, and fellowship; do not sensationalize.
Mystical companion layer remains interpretive and does not replace source-audited restored text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Exodus 3:14 YHWH/Ehyeh asher ehyeh requires deep lexical and reception-history audit.
Exodus 4:21 and hardening motif should receive verb-by-verb audit across Exodus.
Exodus 4:24-26 Zipporah circumcision episode requires source, syntax, and reception-history note.
Exodus land promise / named peoples requires ethical and historical reception audit before later conquest texts.
Exodus 7-12 sign/plague sequence requires ecological, literary, and anti-ethnic-flattening audit.
Exodus 12 Pesach/Passover terminology and calendar reset require Hebrew ritual/source audit.
Exodus 12:38 mixed multitude and 12:49 one Torah for native/sojourner require covenant inclusion audit.
Exodus 14 Sea of Reeds geography and poetic/prose layers require source audit.
Exodus 15 Song of the Sea likely archaic poetic layer requires poetic/source audit.
Exodus 17 Amalek tradition requires ethical and reception-history audit.
Exodus 18 Jethro/Midianite priest wisdom and judicial delegation requires source-history audit.
Exodus 20 Ten Words require Decalogue tradition comparison with Deuteronomy 5.
Exodus 21 slavery/servant laws require ancient Near Eastern context and anti-slavery reception-history audit.
Exodus 21:20-21 corporal punishment language requires explicit anti-abuse audit.
Exodus 22:16-17 seduction/bride-price law requires gender/property ethics audit.
Exodus 22:18 sorcery prohibition requires dangerous reception-history safeguards.
Exodus 23 land/displacement language requires ethical audit before any application.
Exodus 24 covenant ratification/blood language requires ritual-source audit.
next_actions: Continue Exodus with chapters 25-31: tabernacle instructions, ark, table, lampstand, curtains, altar, priestly garments, ordination, incense altar, census, basin, anointing oil, incense, artisans, and Sabbath sign.
